mappings.rationale: Directly covers subsection C on the Mongol Empire established by Genghis Khan and its successor states across Central and western Asia.
Directly relevant to subsections B3 and C2 on trade routes through West and East Turkistan and the commercial networks of Inner Asia.
Directly covers subsection D1 on Tibet including the introduction of Buddhism, the Yellow Hat monastic order, and Chinese overlordship.
Relevant to subsections C2e and D1 on the revival of Buddhism in Mongolia and its central role in Tibetan civilization.
Relevant to subsection C2d on the Golden Horde in eastern Europe, Russian expansion into Siberia, and the waning of nomad power.
Relevant to subsections B3a and C2 on Muslim rule in West Turkistan and the conversion of Mongol successor states to Islam.
Relevant to subsection B5 on Afghanistan under various rulers and C2c on Timur's Timurid dynasty based in Samarkand.
Relevant to the numerous dynasties and khanates discussed throughout the section, from Genghis Khan's successors to the Chagatai and Timurid lines.
Relevant to subsection B2 on Manchurian tribes and their relations with Korea, and the Juchen empire's penetration into Korean territory.
